1.synthesis: Eliphaz opens his second speech with a frontal assault on Job’s intellectual and theological credibility. A wise man, he declares, does not answer with windy knowledge or fill his belly with the east wind (Job 15:2). He accuses Job of doing away with the fear of God and undermining meditation before God (Job 15:4). Then the devastating inversion: Job’s own mouth condemns him, not Eliphaz; Job’s own lips testify against him (Job 15:6). Eliphaz has moved from pastoral concern (ch4–5) to prosecutorial argument: the very complaints Job utters are reframed as self-indicting evidence. The charge is not merely that Job suffers but that his suffering has caused him to speak in ways that reveal a hidden godlessness. This argument represents the friends’ fundamental interpretive move: Job’s protest against God is treated as the real problem, more damning than whatever suffering provoked it.
1.historical_context: The “east wind” (ruach qadim, 15:2) is the hot, destructive desert wind from the east that scorches crops and brings no rain—a figure for speech that is both insubstantial and harmful. Eliphaz’s accusation that Job’s words “do away with the fear of God” (15:4) connects to the book’s framing in chapter 1, where Satan questions precisely whether Job’s fear of God is genuine. Ironically, Eliphaz is making Satan’s argument: the suffering has revealed that Job does not truly fear God. The rhetorical move of turning the accused’s own words against him (“your mouth condemns you, not I,” 15:6) is a classical ancient Near Eastern legal device used in tribunal settings.
1.literary_structure: The second cycle of speeches (ch15–21) is marked by increased sharpness on all sides: the friends are more accusatory, Job more desperate and daring. Eliphaz’s opening gambit—reframing Job’s lament as self-condemning testimony—intensifies the misunderstanding that has been building since chapter 4. The irony for the reader is acute: YHWH has already declared Job’s speech to be right (1:8 establishes Job’s integrity), while Eliphaz treats it as damning. The friends are most confident precisely where they are most wrong. This structural irony is the second cycle’s primary contribution: the gap between the friends’ certainty and the reader’s knowledge of the prologue creates devastating dramatic irony.
1.theological_application: Eliphaz’s charge that Job’s lament reveals godlessness is a mistake communities of faith repeat whenever they interpret honest complaint as faithlessness. The psalmic tradition of lament (Psalm 22, Psalm 88) models what Eliphaz cannot accept: that raw protest against God’s apparent absence or injustice can itself be an act of faith, because it addresses YHWH directly rather than turning away from him. The reframing of lament as evidence of hidden sin is a pastoral failure that re-wounds the suffering rather than accompanying them. Job’s eventual vindication by YHWH (42:7) is a direct repudiation of Eliphaz’s reading: Job spoke rightly, the friends did not.
1.christology: Eliphaz’s claim that Job’s “own mouth condemns him” inverts the logic of the gospel: where Eliphaz uses Job’s honest speech as evidence of guilt, Christ takes on the guilt of those whose speech (and deeds) genuinely condemn them and speaks righteousness on their behalf. The accusatorial logic of the friends—“your own words testify against you”—is the logic of the law as a prosecutorial instrument; the gospel reverses it: “There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus” (Rom 8:1). Christ is the one whose perfect speech and life could not be turned against him, yet who bore the condemnation of those whose words truly did condemn them.

7.pericope_label: Are You the First Man Born?
7.synthesis: Eliphaz challenges Job’s implicit claim to wisdom with a series of withering rhetorical questions: “Are you the first man who was born? Or were you brought forth before the hills?” (Job 15:7). Have you listened in the council of God? Do you limit wisdom to yourself? (Job 15:8). He invokes the authority of the aged and the experience of the tradition: “Both the gray-haired and the aged are among us, older than your father” (Job 15:10). The climax of the argument: God puts no trust even in his holy ones, and the heavens are not pure in his sight; how much less one who is abominable and corrupt, a man who drinks injustice like water (Job 15:15–16). Eliphaz is deploying the highest theological argument available to him: if even the heavenly beings are impure before God, what does Job think he is doing protesting his innocence?
7.historical_context: The question “Are you the first man born?” (15:7) alludes to mythological traditions of a primordial wise man or first man who had access to divine wisdom from the beginning—traditions attested in Mesopotamian literature (Adapa, the apkallu sages). Eliphaz is asking whether Job thinks himself a primordial sage with access to the heavenly council. The same tradition surfaces in Ezekiel 28:12–14 (the prince of Tyre as primordial man in Eden) and in Proverbs 8:25–30 (Wisdom present before the mountains). Eliphaz is ironically correct to gesture toward this tradition: Job’s complaint has indeed pushed toward a kind of cosmic claim about justice, and YHWH’s speeches from the whirlwind will take that claim seriously in a way Eliphaz cannot.
7.literary_structure: The rhetorical questions of vv. 7–10 form a descending challenge: from cosmic primacy to divine council access to senior authority. Eliphaz is systematically dismantling every possible basis for Job’s claims to insight. The theological argument in vv. 14–16 reaches back to Eliphaz’s first speech (4:17–19): no mortal is righteous before God; even the heavenly beings are impure. The argument is structurally sound but contextually devastating—it is used to silence the sufferer rather than illuminate the situation. The contrast between Eliphaz’s confident citations of tradition and Job’s existential crisis is the section’s governing tension.
7.theological_application: Eliphaz’s appeal to the authority of age and tradition (“the gray-haired and the aged are among us”, 15:10) against Job’s fresh experience is a warning about the misuse of orthodoxy as a weapon against those in crisis. Traditional theology is not wrong because it is traditional; it is wrong when deployed to shut down legitimate questions rather than to engage them. The theological claim that all humans are impure before God (15:14–16) is true—Job acknowledges it himself (9:2)—but its application here as a cudgel to silence Job’s protest rather than as a framework within which honest lament can occur is the pastoral failure Eliphaz embodies.
7.christology: The primordial wise man of vv. 7–8, present before the hills and privy to the divine council, is the figure Job is implicitly accused of claiming to be. But this figure—the one truly present at creation’s beginning, present in the divine council, before whom God does not hide wisdom—is precisely the Logos of the Johannine prologue: “In the beginning was the Word…he was in the beginning with God” (John 1:1–2). Christ is the true primordial sage Eliphaz sarcastically denies Job could be. And unlike Job, who legitimately protests his suffering in ignorance of the heavenly council’s proceedings, Christ enters suffering with full knowledge of the Father’s purpose (John 10:18).

17.synthesis: Eliphaz shifts to a long, darkly vivid description of the fate awaiting the wicked, implicitly directed at Job: the wicked man writhes in pain all his days, and the number of years reserved for the ruthless is short (Job 15:20). Terrifying sounds fill his ears; in prosperity the destroyer comes upon him (Job 15:21). He does not believe he will return from darkness, and he is targeted for the sword (Job 15:22). He wanders abroad for bread, knowing that the day of darkness is ready at his hand (Job 15:23). Distress and anguish terrify him; they prevail against him, like a king ready for battle (Job 15:24). The section concludes: “for he has stretched out his hand against God and defies the Almighty” (Job 15:25). Eliphaz is painting a portrait of the wicked that precisely mirrors Job’s own condition—terror, darkness, suffering, wandering—and then attributing it to defiant stretching of the hand against God. The portrait is Job. The accusation is explicit.
17.historical_context: The “woe oracle” genre—extended descriptions of the fate of the wicked—is common in both the wisdom tradition (Prov 5:20–23) and the prophetic tradition (Isa 5:8–23). Eliphaz is operating within the legitimate rhetorical tradition of such oracles, but applying them with pointed intent to Job. The description of the wicked as one who “stretches out his hand against God” (15:25) and “runs against him with a stiff neck, with his thickly bossed shield” (15:26) uses military imagery for the act of cosmic rebellion. The closing agricultural image—“he conceives trouble and gives birth to evil, and his womb prepares deceit” (15:35)—echoes Psalm 7:14 and depicts vice as a generative process.
17.literary_structure: The extended fate-of-the-wicked description (vv. 17–35) occupies the bulk of Eliphaz’s second speech and functions as an implicit accusation through sustained portrait. The technique is that of the rhetorical trap: Eliphaz never says “you are wicked,” but every detail of the portrait fits Job’s condition. The reader who knows the prologue sees what Eliphaz cannot: the portrait is wrong not because the details don’t fit Job’s situation but because Job’s situation is not caused by his wickedness. The very accuracy of the descriptive match—terror, darkness, suffering—makes Eliphaz’s causal conclusion all the more wrong. Accurate description; false causation.
17.theological_application: Eliphaz’s wicked-man portrait applied to Job illustrates a persistent theological error: confusing the condition of the guilty with the condition of the innocent who suffer the same symptoms. The description of the wicked man in pain (vv. 20–24) could equally describe the righteous sufferer; the difference is not the phenomenology of suffering but its cause. Communities shaped by Job are trained to resist the move from “this person suffers as the wicked do” to “this person suffers because they are wicked.” The gospel itself makes this error impossible at the highest level: the righteous Son of God suffers the curse deserved by the wicked, precisely to liberate the wicked from it. Suffering and guilt are not correlated in the way Eliphaz insists.
17.christology: The portrait of the wicked man who “stretches out his hand against God” (15:25) and suffers the consequences is, in Eliphaz’s application, a portrait of Job. But in the light of the cross, a deeper portrait emerges: the suffering described here—terror, darkness, the sense of being targeted, wandering for bread—is what Christ bore in his passion, not because he was wicked but because he bore the wicked’s fate on their behalf. “He made him to be sin who knew no sin” (2 Cor 5:21): the one who did not stretch his hand against God bore the fate of those who did. The fate-of-the-wicked oracle, fulfilled in the passion, is where Eliphaz’s darkest rhetoric finds its true subject.
